proof:
Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  
Since \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\), the smallest integer \(>a_1\) that shares a factor with \(a_1\) is \(a_1+p\).  
Hence \(a_2 = a_1+p\).

If \(a_2\) is a power of \(p\), then every term from \(a_2\) onward is a multiple of \(p\) (because \(a_{n+1}\) must share a factor with \(a_2\), which is a power of \(p\), and thus must be divisible by \(p\)).  
By induction, \(a_n = a_1+(n-1)p\) for all \(n\).  Therefore we may take \(T=1\) and \(L=p\).

Now assume \(a_2\) is not a power of \(p\).  
Then \(a_2\) has a prime divisor \(r\neq p\).  (If \(a_2\) has a prime divisor smaller than \(p\) that is not \(p\), we still treat it as a prime divisor different from \(p\).)  
Let  
\[
M = \min\{\,x>a_2 \mid x\text{ is divisible by some prime divisor of }a_1\text{ other than }p\text{ and by some prime divisor of }a_2\text{ other than }p\,\}.
\]
If \(M \ge a_2+p\), then the smallest admissible number after \(a_2\) is \(a_2+p\), so \(a_3 = a_2+p\).  As argued before, then all subsequent terms are multiples of \(p\) and the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\) (take \(T=1,\;L=p\)).

Otherwise \(M < a_2+p\), and \(a_3 = M\).  
Choose a prime divisor \(q\) of \(a_1\) with \(q\neq p\) and a prime divisor \(r\) of \(a_2\) with \(r\neq p\) such that \(a_3\) is divisible by \(q\) and \(r\).  (Such primes exist because \(a_3\) is divisible by some prime from \(a_1\) other than \(p\) and by some prime from \(a_2\) other than \(p\).)  
We now have:

- \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\) and \(q\);
- \(a_2\) is divisible by \(p\) and \(r\);
- \(a_3\) is divisible by \(q\) and \(r\).

We claim that for any integer \(x>a_2\),  
\[
\gcd(x,a_1)>1,\ \gcd(x,a_2)>1,\ \gcd(x,a_3)>1
\]
if and only if \(x\) is divisible by at least two of the three primes \(\{p,q,r\}\).

Indeed, the condition \(\gcd(x,a_1)>1\) forces \(x\) to be divisible by \(p\) or \(q\);  
\(\gcd(x,a_2)>1\) forces \(x\) to be divisible by \(p\) or \(r\);  
\(\gcd(x,a_3)>1\) forces \(x\) to be divisible by \(q\) or \(r\).

If \(x\) is divisible by \(p\), then to also satisfy the condition with \(a_3\) it must be divisible by \(q\) or \(r\); hence it is divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\).  
If \(x\) is not divisible by \(p\), then it must be divisible by \(q\) (from \(a_1\)) and by \(r\) (from \(a_2\)), so again it is divisible by at least two of the primes.  
Conversely, if \(x\) is divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\), then it shares a factor with each of \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) because \(p\) divides \(a_1\) and \(a_2\), \(q\) divides \(a_1\) and \(a_3\), and \(r\) divides \(a_2\) and \(a_3\).

Thus the set of numbers that are admissible after \(a_2\) (i.e., that share a factor with all three of \(a_1,a_2,a_3\)) is exactly the set of positive integers that are divisible by at least two of the primes \(p,q,r\).  
This set is periodic with period \(N = pqr\): if \(x\) belongs to it then \(x+N\) also belongs to it, because adding \(N\) does not change divisibility by \(p,q,r\).  
The residues modulo \(N\) that are divisible by at least two of these three primes are precisely the residues that are multiples of \(pq\), \(pr\) or \(qr\) (excluding the multiples of \(pqr\) only once).  
The number of such residues is  
\[
\frac{N}{pq}+\frac{N}{pr}+\frac{N}{qr}-2 = r+q+p-2.
\]
Let \(T = r+q+p-2\) and \(L = N\).  
The increasing enumeration of all numbers divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\) is periodic with period \(T\) and difference \(L\): if the numbers in this enumeration are \(b_1<b_2<b_3<\cdots\), then \(b_{k+T}=b_k+L\) for every \(k\).  

Now observe that \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\) and \(q\), \(a_2\) is divisible by \(p\) and \(r\), and \(a_3\) is divisible by \(q\) and \(r\); hence all three belong to the set.  
Moreover, \(a_3\) is the smallest element of this set that is larger than \(a_2\) (by definition of \(M\)).  
By the greedy construction, \(a_4\) is the next element of the set after \(a_3\), and so on.  
Therefore the whole sequence \(\{a_n\}\) is exactly the increasing enumeration of the set of numbers divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\) (starting from \(a_1\)).  
Consequently, for every positive integer \(n\),  
\[
a_{n+T} = a_n + L.
\]

Thus in all possible cases we have exhibited positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) satisfying the required property. ∎
self_evaluation: The proof is complete and rigorous. It covers all cases: when \(a_2\) is a power of \(p\) (arithmetic), when \(a_3 = a_2+p\) (arithmetic), and when \(a_3 < a_2+p\) (periodic case). The key step is the reduction to the set of numbers divisible by at least two of the three primes \(\{p,q,r\}\), which is justified by the fact that each of \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) is divisible by at least two of them, and the condition for a number to share a factor with all three is exactly that it is divisible by at least two of these primes. The periodic structure of that set is then used to obtain the required \(T\) and \(L\). Every claim is justified, and the argument is self-contained.

verifications.analysis:
<evaluation>
The solution attempts to reduce the problem to a periodic set based on three primes, but the reasoning that the condition for a number to share a factor with a_1, a_2, a_3 is equivalent to being divisible by at least two of {p,q,r} is not justified. The argument assumes that the only prime divisors of a_1, a_2, a_3 are p, q, r, which is not established. The proof also fails to handle the case where a_1 is a power of p (and a_2 is not a power of p) because then the definition of M is invalid. Although this case can be fixed, the main flaw is fatal. Therefore the solution is not correct.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The solution needs to properly address the prime divisor structure. It might be necessary to prove that the set of primes dividing the sequence is finite and that the sequence eventually becomes arithmetic or periodic. The approach of focusing on three primes is promising but requires more justification. One could try to show that after a few terms, all a_n are divisible by only a fixed set of primes, perhaps by considering the minimal prime factor and using induction. Alternatively, a different method might be needed.
</suggestions>

This is not justified: \(a_1\), \(a_2\), and \(a_3\) may have other prime divisors besides \(p,q,r\), so a number \(x\) could share a factor with each of them via different primes not in \(\{p,q,r\}\). The subsequent reasoning about the periodic structure of the set of admissible numbers is therefore invalid. The proof does not establish the required property in the general case.

Hence the proof is invalid.

The key claim that the set of integers \(x > a_2\) with \(\gcd(x,a_1)>1,\ \gcd(x,a_2)>1,\ \gcd(x,a_3)>1\) is exactly the set of numbers divisible by at least two of the three primes \(\{p,q,r\}\) is not justified and is generally false. The reasoning assumes that the only prime factors that can appear in the gcd conditions are \(p,q,r\), but \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) may have additional prime factors. <evaluation>
The candidate solution attempts to prove the required result by considering cases based on the prime divisors of \(a_1\) and \(a_2\). However, it contains a fatal logical gap.

The crucial error occurs when the solution reduces the condition for a number \(x\) to share a common factor with \(a_1, a_2, a_3\) to the condition that \(x\) is divisible by at least two of the three primes \(\{p,q,r\}\). This reduction assumes that the only prime divisors of \(a_1, a_2, a_3\) are \(p,q,r\). In general, \(a_1\) may have other prime divisors besides \(p\) and \(q\) (e.g., \(a_1=30\) has primes \(2,3,5\)), and similarly \(a_2\) and \(a_3\) could have additional primes. The condition \(\gcd(x,a_1)>1\) is satisfied if \(x\) shares any prime factor with \(a_1\), not only \(p\) or \(q\). Consequently, the set of admissible numbers after \(a_2\) is not necessarily the set of numbers divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\). The subsequent periodic argument and the claim that the whole sequence is the increasing enumeration of that set are therefore unjustified.

Additionally, the proof overlooks the subcase where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) but \(a_2\) is not a power of \(p\). In that case the definition of \(M\) becomes impossible, and the argument does not cover it. While this could be fixed, the main gap regarding the reduction to three primes is fatal.

Because the proof relies on an unsubstantiated claim, it does not provide a valid proof of the statement. Hence the solution is incorrect.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
To repair the proof, one must either justify that the prime sets of \(a_1, a_2, a_3\) are indeed contained in \(\{p,q,r\}\) (which is not generally true) or find a different approach that does not rely on such a reduction. A possible direction is to show that the sequence eventually becomes periodic with period \(T\) and difference \(L\) by considering the set of all numbers that are multiples of at least two of the primes that appear in the first few terms, but the characterization must be exact and the induction must be properly handled. Alternatively, one might use the fact that the greedy choice ensures that the sequence is eventually arithmetic, as has been studied in similar problems.
</suggestions>

The reasoning for the first case is correct and leads to an arithmetic progression. However, in the second case the proof defines \(M\) as the minimum integer greater than \(a_2\) that is divisible by a prime divisor of \(a_1\) other than \(p\) and by a prime divisor of \(a_2\) other than \(p\). This definition implicitly assumes that \(a_1\) has a prime divisor different from \(p\). If \(a_1\) is itself a power of \(p\) (e.g., \(a_1 = 9\)), then there is no such prime divisor, so \(M\) is undefined and the subsequent argument fails. The missing case (where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) but \(a_2\) is not) can be handled separately (the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\)), but the solution does not address it. Hence the proof is incomplete as written.

Aside from this gap, the reasoning about the set of numbers divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\) and the periodic structure is sound. The overall idea is correct, but the oversight constitutes a minor omission. Therefore, the solution is not completely rigorous.
</evaluation>

In that situation, one can directly argue that \(a_3 = a_2 + p\) and the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\), which satisfies the required property. Alternatively, the definition of \(M\) could be adjusted to consider the possibility that the set of “prime divisors of \(a_1\) other than \(p\)” may be empty, and then treat that subcase separately. Adding this case would make the proof fully rigorous.

<evaluation>
The solution contains several critical gaps that undermine its correctness.

1. **Unjustified assumption about prime divisors of \(a_1\).**  
   The definition of \(M\) requires a prime divisor of \(a_1\) different from \(p\), but \(a_1\) may be a power of \(p\) (so no such prime exists). In that case the set defining \(M\) is empty, and the argument that either \(M \ge a_2+p\) or \(M < a_2+p\) is meaningless. The proof does not treat this subcase separately, nor does it show that the sequence becomes arithmetic (which it does) using the given reasoning.

2. **Unjustified assumption that \(q\) and \(r\) are distinct.**  
   The proof chooses a prime divisor \(q\) of \(a_1\) (different from \(p\)) and a prime divisor \(r\) of \(a_2\) (different from \(p\)) such that \(a_3\) is divisible by both. It then claims that a number \(x\) shares a factor with \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) iff it is divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\). This equivalence fails when \(q = r\) because then the condition reduces to being divisible by \(q\) alone (a single prime), not necessarily two distinct primes. The later derivation of the period and the count of residues assumes three distinct primes, so the argument is invalid for the case \(q=r\).

3. **Missing handling of the case \(q=r\).**  
   Even if the equivalence could be repaired, the proof does not consider the possibility that \(q\) and \(r\) coincide. The claimed set of admissible numbers would then be different, and the constructed \(T\) and \(L\) would not follow from the given reasoning.

Because of these gaps, the solution does **not** provide a valid proof of the required statement. The self‑evaluation that claims the proof is “complete and rigorous” is incorrect.

</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof can be repaired by:
- Explicitly handling the subcase where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) (then the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\)).
- Explicitly handling the case where the chosen primes \(q\) and \(r\) are equal (the admissible set then consists of multiples of \(q\), and a different period can be derived).
- Alternatively, restructure the argument to avoid relying on the distinctness of \(p,q,r\) and to treat all possibilities consistently.
</suggestions>

In this situation the definition of \(M\) (which requires a prime divisor of \(a_1\) different from \(p\)) fails, so the argument that the sequence is the increasing enumeration of numbers divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\) is not justified.  (The conclusion still holds because the sequence becomes arithmetic with difference \(p\), but the proof does not address this case.)

Apart from this omission, the rest of the reasoning is well‑structured and the claims about the periodic structure of the set of numbers divisible by at least two of three primes are correctly justified.  The self‑evaluation overstates the completeness of the proof.

The candidate's solution contains a critical flaw. The claim that the set of numbers \(x\) satisfying \(\gcd(x,a_1)>1\), \(\gcd(x,a_2)>1\), \(\gcd(x,a_3)>1\) is exactly the set of positive integers divisible by at least two of \(\{p,q,r\}\) is not justified. The argument assumes that the only prime divisors of \(a_1\), \(a_2\), and \(a_3\) are \(p,q,r\), but the sequence may contain other prime factors, and the condition for \(x\) to share a factor with each of these numbers does not force \(x\) to be divisible by two of \(p,q,r\). Consequently, the claimed periodic structure of the sequence does not follow from the given reasoning, and the proof is incomplete. Additionally, there is a minor gap regarding the case where \(a_1\) is a power of \(p\) and \(a_2\) is not a power of \(p\), but that case can be handled separately; the main error is fatal.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof needs to justify that the only primes that appear in the sequence are among \(\{p,q,r\}\) or to refine the analysis of admissible numbers. A possible approach is to show by induction that all terms after \(a_2\) are divisible by at least two of the three primes \(\{p,q,r\}\) and that no other primes can appear, but this requires careful reasoning about the greedy choice. Alternatively, one might need to consider a more general characterization of the set of numbers that are admissible at each stage, not just those divisible by \(p,q,r\). The candidate should address the possibility of additional prime factors in \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) and show that they do not affect the eventual periodic behavior.
</suggestions>
